response_text: OFFICE OF INSPECTOR GENERAL U.S. Department of Homeland Security Washington, DC 20528 | www.oig.dhs.gov December 12, [address] Boston, MA 02115 SENT VIA EMAIL TO: [email] Subject: Freedom of Information Act Appeal; Appeal No. 2025-IGAP-00002; Request No. 2024-IGFO-00066 Dear Mr. Free: This letter is in response to your administrative appeal, dated October 9, 2024, and received by the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Office of Inspector General (OIG) on November 15, 2024. The DHS OIG FOIA Office subsequently acknowledged receipt of your appeal and assigned it number 2025-IGAP-00002 for tracking purposes. Administrative Record Your appeal pertains to a FOIA request that you submitted via email to the DHS OIG FOIA Office on February 21, 2024, seeking “[t]he investigative records pertaining to the June 2019 sexual abuse allegation against Brian Louis Robinson by a woman detained for ICE at the Baker County Detention Center in Macclenny, Florida.” In your request, you noted that Mr. Robinson “was convicted by a jury in mid-February 2024 on all counts and is awaiting sentencing.” In addition, your request sought a fee waiver and expedited processing. This request was received, acknowledged, and assigned tracking number 2024-IGFO00066 by the DHS OIG FOIA Office on February 23, 2024. In its acknowledgement letter, the DHS OIG FOIA Office advised you that your request for a fee waiver was granted and that your request for expedited processing was denied. On October 8, 2024, the DHS OIG FOIA Office sent a final response letter advising the following: Please be advised that absent (1) the named individuals’ consent, (2) an official acknowledgement of accusations against or an investigation of the named individual, or (3) an overriding public interest, the acknowledgement of the existence of records sought would constitute a clearly unwarranted invasion of personal privacy. Accordingly, the OIG will neither confirm nor deny the existence of responsive records pursuant to Exemptions 6 and 7(C) of the FOIA, 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(6); 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(7)(C). This response should not be taken as an indication that such records do or do not exist. Rather, it is OIG’s standard response to requesters seeking information or records pertaining to third parties. Appeal and Determination Your appeal appears to contest the agency’s “GLOMAR” denial. Following a review of your appeal and the administrative record, I am remanding FOIA request 2024-IGFO00066 to the DHS OIG FOIA Office to search for, process, and release any responsive, non-exempt records to you. Should you need assistance with your request, you may contact DHS OIG’s FOIA Public Liaison. You may also seek dispute resolution services from the FOIA Public Liaison. You may contact DHS OIG’s FOIA Public Liaison at [email]. If you are unable to resolve your FOIA dispute through the FOIA Public Liaison, the Office of Government Information Services (OGIS), offers mediation services to help resolve disputes between FOIA requesters and Federal agencies. If you are requesting access to your own records (which is considered a Privacy Act request) you should know that OGIS does not have the authority to handle requests made under the Privacy Act of 1974. Using OGIS services does not affect your right to pursue litigation. The contact information for OGIS is: Office of Government Information Services National Archives and Records Administration [address] – OGIS College Park, MD 20740-6001 E-mail: [email] Telephone: [phone] Fax: [phone] Toll-free: [phone] Should you have any questions regarding this appeal adjudication, please contact this office at [email]. In the subject line of the email, include the word 2 “appeal,” your appeal number, which is 2025-IGAP-00002 and the FOIA case number, which is 2024-IGFO-00066. Sincerely, Digitally signed by CECILIA O CECILIA O BESSEE BESSEE Date: 2024.12.12 13:58:41 -05'00' Cecilia O. Bessee Deputy Counsel to the Inspector General Office of the Inspector General U.S. Department of Homeland Security 3

request_text: the following records: The investigative file for OSHA case number 21-2116-ATL (20). The release OSHA distributed regarding this case read as follows: Department of Labor Logo OSHA News Release - Region 4 U.S. Department of Labor January 19, 2022 US Department of Labor cites Florida company after worker clearing brush dies as temperatures neared 100 degrees in national forest OSHA finds Earthbalance Corp. exposed workers to dangers of heat exposure NORTH PORT, FL – Working in the recesses of Apalachicola National Forest on a July day as temperatures neared 100 degrees, the supervisor of two crews hired to clear invasive plants saw one 42-year-old worker was sweating heavily, his hands were trembling, and he seemed confused, unable to respond to commands. The worker rested while other employees finished their tasks. Only 30 minutes later, the supervisor returned to the man finding him unresponsive. Without a cell phone signal, workers had to get help from a ranger station, 14 miles away from their job site. By the time an ambulance became available, the worker had stopped breathing and responders found no pulse. Soon after transport to a hospital, doctors pronounced him dead. An investigation by the U.S. Department of Labor's Occupational Safety and Health Administration of the July 30 incident underscores the need for employers to understand the dangers of hazardous heat in the workplace, and to take action to protect workers from the serious potential consequences. OSHA investigators found Earthbalance Corp., a North Point-based environmental restoration firm, exposed workers to hazards related to high ambient heat and failed to adequately train a person to perform first aid, and ensure they were available to render assistance in heat-related emergencies. The company faces $24,576 in proposed penalties. "Earthbalance Corp. failed to protect their employees from the dangers from extreme temperatures and take actions that may have prevented a worker's death," said OSHA Acting Area Director Nolan Houser in Jacksonville, Florida. "We encourage employers to reach out to the agency's staff and use our free resources for recognizing and mitigating heat-related hazards to ensure their workers have every opportunity to return home safely at the end of the day." Here is a link to the OSHA press release regarding this case: https://www.osha.gov/news/newsreleases/region4/01192022 Atlanta (Region 4) is the likely custodian, based on this press release. Requestor Information: I make this request on behalf of Jeremy Young, producer with Al Jazeera English News, and MuckRock News, who are journalists primarily engaged in the dissemination of information and will make this information freely available to the public. We respectfully seek a fee waiver for this request. In addition, because this request forms part of the reporting on the increasingly deadly legal landscape surrounding heat related deaths of outdoor workers, and an urgent need to inform the public of the consequences of state-level legislation shortening or eliminating water and rest break periods for outdoor workers, we respectfully request expedited processing and I certify that the foregoing statements are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and understanding pursuant to 28 USC 1746. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NEYQJ4r3osY The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes. In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not. Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.
response_text: Occupational Safety and Health Administration Atlanta Regional Office Sam Nunn Atlanta Federal Center [address], SW, Room 6T50 Atlanta, Georgia 30303 July 8, [address] Boston, MA 02115 [email] Your reference: 21-2116-ATL (20) Subject: FOIA 2024-F-10559 Dear Sir or madame: This decision is in response to your Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request dated June 14, 2024 and received in our office on June 14, 2024 requesting records concerning Earthbalance Corp. - Inspection #1545586, Press Release Number: 21-2116-ATL (20). We located the records you seek and conducted a review of the material you requested. After reviewing this information, and narrowing your request, we have made the following release determination. Under FOIA, “[a]ny reasonably segregable portion of a record shall be provided to any person requesting such record after deletion of the portions which are exempt . . . .” 5 U.S.C. § 552(b). Where we have withheld pages entirely, it is because those pages contain no reasonably segregable information that may be released without creating the harm the indicated exemptions were designed to prevent. There are a total of 57 pages being released. We have determined the following pages may be released in full: 41 pages. We have determined the following pages may be released with redaction : 12 pages with personal identifying information redacted pursuant to Exemption 7(C). 2 pages with confidential business information redacted pursuant to Exemption 4. 2 pages with personal identifying information redacted pursuant to Exemption 7(C) Exemption 7(D) disclosure could reasonably be expected to identify persons who provide information to the government in confidence: witness statements. Exemption 4 protects “trade secrets and commercial or financial information obtained from a person [that is] privileged or confidential.” 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(4). This exemption is intended to protect two categories of information in agency records: (1) trade secrets; and (2) certain confidential or privileged commercial information. We are withholding certain privileged or confidential information pursuant to Exemption 4. When applying this part of exemption 4, the terms “commercial or financial” should not be narrowly construed to include proprietary information only. Rather, they should be given their ordinary meaning. Under Exemption 4, information that is submitted to the government is categorically protected from disclosure provided it is not customarily and actually disclosed to the public by the submitter and was submitted to the government under an assurance of confidentiality. See Food Mktg. Inst. v. Argus Leader Media, 139 S. Ct. 915 (2019). For this reason, this information is protected by Exemption 4. Exemption 5 of FOIA allows an agency to withhold “inter-agency or intra-agency” information that would not be available to a party in litigation with the agency. 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(5). As an initial matter, exemption 5 requires the agency to determine whether the documents requested are “normally privileged in the civil discovery context.” Nat’l Labor Relations Bd. v. Sears, Roebuck & Co., 421 U.S. 132, 149 (1975). One privilege available to government agencies is the deliberative process privilege. This privilege protects confidential intra-agency opinions of governmental personnel whose disclosure “would be injurious to the consultative functions of government.” This privilege, unique to the government, ensures that personnel within an agency feel free to provide decision makers with their opinions and recommendations without fear of later being subject to public criticism. Also, the privilege protects against misleading the public by disseminating material suggesting reasons for a course of action that may not have been the ultimate reasons for the agency’s action. Exemption 5 of FOIA allows an agency to withhold “inter-agency or intra-agency” information that would not be available to a party in litigation with the agency. 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(5). As an initial matter, Exemption 5 requires the agency to determine whether the documents requested are normally privileged in the civil discovery context. This privilege protects confidential communications between a government attorney and a client agency that has sought the attorney’s advice. The privilege applies both to facts divulged by a client to the attorney and to opinions given by the attorney to the client based upon those facts. Federal agencies – no less than individuals and corporations – require confidential legal advice from their attorneys to function effectively. We have withheld the noted materials pursuant to Exemption 5’s attorneyclient privilege because they are reflective of attorneys’ opinions and advice provided to the Department. Exemption 6 permits the withholding of information contained in “personnel and medical files and similar files the disclosure of which would constitute a clearly unwarranted invasion of personal privacy.” 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(6). The Supreme Court has ruled that the term “similar files” encompasses any government record that concerns a particular individual; the term is not limited to records contained in personnel or medical files. See, e.g., Dep’t of State v. Washington Post, 456 U.S. 595 (1982). We find that release of personal identifying information withheld here reasonably could be expected to have a negative impact on an individual’s privacy. Exemption 7(C) permits an agency to withhold information contained in files compiled for law enforcement purposes if production “could reasonably be expected to constitute an unwarranted invasion of personal privacy.” 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(7)(C). Thus, the purpose of Exemption 7(C) is to protect the privacy of any person mentioned in law enforcement records. In determining whether a protected privacy interest exists, we must evaluate not only the nature of the personal information found in the records, but also whether release of that information to the general public could affect that individual adversely. Thus, we must consider whether release of even seemingly innocuous personal information could lead to the harassment or annoyance of an individual through unsolicited inquiries. We find that release of personal identifying information withheld here reasonably could be expected to have a negative impact on an individual’s privacy. Exemption 7(D) protects from disclosure information that reasonably could be expected to identify persons or entities providing data to the government in confidence or under circumstances implying confidentiality. 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(7)(D). The applicability of Exemption 7(D) does not end with termination of an inspection because the potential harm or scrutiny that a confidential informant may be subjected is not dependent upon the phase of an inspection. Rather, potential harm may result from the mere fact that an individual communicated with the government. We have withheld the noted materials pursuant to Exemption 7(D) to protect from disclosure information that reasonably could be expected to identify persons or entities providing data to the government in confidence or under circumstances implying confidentiality. with its provisions” and grants agency discretion to determine whether to grant certification or attestation under seal of a requested record. See also Jimenez v. Executive Office for U.S. Attorneys, 764 F.Supp.2d 174, 182 (D.D.C. 2011). There were no fees associated with this request. You have the right to appeal this decision with the Solicitor of Labor within 90 days from the date of this letter. The appeal must state, in writing, the grounds for the appeal, including any supporting statements or arguments. The appeal should also include a copy of your initial request and a copy of this letter. If you appeal, you may mail your appeal to: Solicitor of Labor, U.S. Department of Labor, Room N-2420, [address], N.W., Washington, D.C. 20210 or fax your appeal to ([phone]. Alternatively, you may email your appeal to [email]; appeals submitted to any other email address will not be accepted. The envelope (if mailed), subject line (if emailed), or fax cover sheet (if faxed), and the letter indicating the grounds for appeal, should be clearly marked: “Freedom of Information Act Appeal.” In addition to filing an appeal, you may contact the Department’s FOIA Public Liaison, Thomas G. Hicks, Sr. at ([phone] or [email] for assistance in resolving disputes. You also may contact the Office of Government Information Services (OGIS) for assistance. OGIS offers mediation services to resolve disputes between FOIA requesters and federal agencies as a non-exclusive alternative to litigation. Using OGIS services does not affect your right to pursue litigation. You may mail OGIS at the Office of Government Information Services, National Archives and Records Administration, [address] – OGIS, College Park, MD 20740-6001. Alternatively, you may email or contact OGIS through its website at: [email]; Web: https://ogis.archives.gov. Finally, you can call or fax OGIS at: telephone: ([phone]; fax: ([phone]; toll-free: [phone]. It is also important to note that the services offered by OGIS, is not an alternative to filing an administrative FOIA appeal. Respectfully, Carla T. Smith Government Information Specialist OSHA Region IV Administrative Programs/FOIA

request_text: Dear Tennessee Valley Authority, On behalf of myself and Fiat Fiendum, I hereby request the following records: 1. The one email in your chief FOIA officer's email inbox or equivalent which was received nearest and before October 19, 2024, midnight Eastern time, regardless of subject matter. 2. The one email in your chief FOIA officer's email spam folder or equivalent which was received nearest and before October 19, 2024, midnight Eastern time, regardless of subject matter. 3. The one email in your chief FOIA officer's sent email folder or equivalent which was sent nearest and before October 19, 2024, midnight Eastern time, regardless of subject matter. 4. The total full-time equivalent count of people who work for your FOIA office as of October 19, 2024. This request is expressly limited to Tennessee Valley Authority's own FOIA office. Please do not refer it to any other agency or division. This request is expressly not a Privacy Act request, and no PA waiver will be provided. Please provide the email records in EML, Maildir, MBOX, MIX, MSG, or PST format only — not PDF, Word, image, etc — including all metadata (e.g. full email headers as received/sent) and attachments, without alteration other than narrowly tailored electronic redaction of specifically claimed exemptions that do not affect any part of non exempt information (and, in particular, without rasterization). response_text: Federal Communications Commission Washington, D.C. 20554 March 19, 2025 VIA ELECTRONIC MAIL Ben Welsh MuckRock News DEPT MR176440 Boston, MA 02115 [email] Re: FOIA Control No. 2025-000247 Mr. Welsh: This letter responds to the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request you submitted to the Federal Communications Commission (FCC or Commission) on November 18, 2024. Through your request, you seek, for the time period January 1, 2023, through the date of the search: “[a]ny emails that contain the word Elon or the word Musk sent by . . . [Chairman] Brendan Carr, Chief of Staff Greg Watson, Legal Advisor Danielle Thumann, Legal Advisor Arpan A. Sura, [and] Assistant Drema Johnson.” 1 Your request has been assigned FOIA Control No. 2025-000247 and was referred to the Office of General Counsel. In response to your request, Commission staff undertook a search for responsive records, locating one record which is being withheld in full under FOIA Exemption 5, which protects certain interagency and intra-agency records that are normally considered privileged in the civil discovery context. 2 Exemption 5 encompasses a deliberative process privilege intended to “prevent injury to the quality of agency decisions.” 3 To fall within the scope of this privilege the agency records must be both predecisional and deliberative. 4 Predecisional records must have been “prepared in order to assist an agency decision maker in arriving at his decision.” 5 Deliberative records must be such that their disclosure “would expose an agency’s decisionmaking process in such a way as to discourage candid discussion within the agency and thereby undermine the agency’s ability to perform its functions.” 6 As applied to the instant matter, we are withholding the responsive record, where the term “Elon” is FOIA Control No. 2025-000247 (Nov. 18, 2024) (internal quotations omitted). 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(5) 3 NLRB v. Sears Roebuck & Co., 421 U.S. 132, 150-51 (1975). 4 Id. at 151-52. 5 Formaldehyde Inst. v. Dep’t of Health and Human Servs., 889 F.2d 1118, 1122 (D.C. Cir. 1989); see also Coastal States Gas Corp. v. Dep’t of Energy, 617 F.2d 854, 866 (D.C. Cir. 1980) (“In deciding whether a document should be protected by the privilege we look to whether the document is . . . generated before the adoption of an agency policy and whether . . . it reflects the give-and-take of the consultative process. The exemption thus covers recommendations, draft documents, proposals, suggestions, and other subjective documents . . .”). 6 Formaldehyde Inst., 889 F.2d at 1122 (quoting Dudman Commc’ns Corp. v. Dep’t of the Air Force, 815 F.2d 1565, 1568 (D.C. Cir. 1987)). 1 2 only mentioned incidentally, pursuant to the deliberative process privilege insofar as the record reflects predecisional, deliberative communications between the Chairman and his staff, including recommendations from staff members. We have determined that disclosure of this material would cause specific reasonably foreseeable harms to the deliberative process, which Exemption 5 is intended to protect. As noted, the withheld material consists of deliberations involving the advice and recommendations of junior staff to more senior staff, public exposure of which would foreseeably cause hesitation in offering candid opinions to superiors, resulting in self-censorship that would degrade the quality of agency decisions. We are required by both the FOIA and the Commission’s own rules to charge requesters certain fees associated with the costs of searching for, reviewing, and duplicating the sought after information. 7 To calculate the appropriate fee, requesters are classified as: (1) commercial use requesters; (2) educational requesters, non-commercial scientific organizations, or representatives of the news media; or (3) all other requesters. 8 Pursuant to section 0.466(a)(5)-(7) of the Commission’s rules, you have been classified as category (2), “educational requesters, non-commercial scientific organizations, or representatives of the news media.” 9 As an “educational requester, non-commercial scientific organization, or representative of the news media,” the Commission assesses charges to recover the cost of reproducing the records requested, excluding the cost of reproducing the first 100 pages. Given that no records are being produced, no fees will be assessed in this matter. If you consider this to be a denial of your FOIA request, you may seek review by filing an application for review with the Office of General Counsel. An application for review must be received by the Commission within 90 calendar days of the date of this letter. 10 You may file an application for review by mailing the application to Federal Communications Commission, Office of General Counsel, [address] NE, Washington, DC 20554, or you may file your application for review electronically by e-mailing it to [email]. Please caption the envelope (or subject line, if via e-mail) and the application itself as “Review of Freedom of Information Action.” If you would like to discuss this response before filing an application for review to attempt to resolve your dispute without going through the appeals process, you may contact the Commission’s FOIA Public Liaison for assistance at: FOIA Public Liaison Federal Communications Commission Office of the Managing Director Performance Evaluation and Records Management [address] NE, Washington, DC 20554 [phone] See 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(4)(A); 47 CFR § 0.470. 47 CFR § 0.470. 9 47 CFR § 0.466(a)(5)-(7). 10 47 CFR §§ 0.461(j), 1.115; 47 CFR § 1.7 (documents are considered filed with the Commission upon their receipt at the location designated by the Commission). 7 8 [email] If you are unable to resolve your FOIA dispute through the Commission’s FOIA Public Liaison, the Office of Government Information Services (OGIS), the Federal FOIA Ombudsman’s office, offers mediation services to help resolve disputes between FOIA requesters and Federal agencies. The contact information for OGIS is: Office of Government Information Services National Archives and Records Administration [address]–OGIS College Park, MD 20740-6001 [phone] [phone] [email] https://www.archives.gov/ogis Sincerely, /s/ Christopher Santini Christopher Santini Special Counsel Office of General Counsel cc: FCC FOIA Office

response_text: Dear Daniel Melchior: This letter is to acknowledge your Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request of January 7, 2025, in which you requested "copies of all completed inspection files for the construction industry, conducted by OSHA in the state of Georgia from Jan 1, 2024 to Dec. 31, 2024. Please include inspection forms, narratives, citations, and penalty information where applicable." Your request was received in our office and assigned FOIA tracking number 2025-F-05345. The OSHA Atlanta Region is unable to respond to your request within the time limits established by the FOIA. Our ability to process your request is delayed because of the voluminous nature of your request, which requires additional time to enable us to determine which records or portions thereof may be released. Additionally, we are experiencing a backlog of requests. Please be advised however, that your request is being handled as equitably as possible and that all documents that can be released will be made available at the earliest possible date. We will keep you informed of our progress. The OSHA Atlanta East, Atlanta West, and Savannah Area Offices completed 323 inspections in the state of Georgia in calendar year 2024. See the attached spreadsheet for the complete listing of those inspections. For contested cases, the proceedings are not concluded and release of the records in these proceedings could reasonably be expected to reveal OSHA's case prematurely, and otherwise interfere with OSHA's ability to effectively enforce the law. As a result, we are withholding the records you seek pursuant to exemption 7(A) of FOIA. To date we have no knowledge of how long the litigation process will take. Information included in the Inspection Report, Citations, and Penalty Information, is available through the Inspection Information Search on the OSHA website at https://www.osha.gov/ords/imis/inspectionNr.html, by entering the inspection number. The following information is publicly available on the website: Inspection Nr Report ID Date Opened Site Address Mailing Address Union Status SIC NAICS Inspection Type Scope Advanced Notice Ownership Safety/Health Close Conference Emphasis Case Closed Related Activity (Complaint/Referral) Case Status Violation Summary Violation Items - Information on the citation can be found by clicking on the Citation ID (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-rTa48BHOYc) If you would like to proceed with your request for the Inspection Forms, Narrative, Citations and Penalty information, please respond to this email to confirm. Due to the backlog, it may take up to 6 months to complete your request. Please contact me via email at [email]<mailto:[email]>, if you have any questions regarding your request. Sincerely, Christi Griffin Deputy Regional Administrator OSHA Atlanta Region [address]. SW, Room 6T50 Atlanta, GA 30303 ([phone]

request_text: the following records: Reports show that ICE, HSI, and DEA detained several people across Puerto Rico on or around Sunday, January 26, 2024 as part of an immigration raid in compliance with an Executive Order signed by President Trump: https://www.elnuevodia.com/noticias/seguridad/notas/directora-del-hsi-niega-que-migrantes-detenidos-en-operativo-federal-contaran-con-documentacion-oficial/ https://www.metro.pr/noticias/2025/01/27/realizan-redada-en-barrio-obrero-para-detener-extranjeros-indocumentados/ Therefore, I request the following documents: 1) Any after-action reports or assessments prepared following the operation that took place on or around Sunday, January 26, 2024 across Puerto Rico. 2) Memoranda, emails, or other communications discussing the planning, execution, or outcomes of the operation that took place on or around Sunday, January 26, 2024 across Puerto Rico. 3) Operational plans or directives related to the raid that took place on or around Sunday, January 26, 2024 across Puerto Rico. 4) Any records documenting the number of individuals detained, arrested, or questioned during the operation that took place on or around Sunday, January 26, 2024 across Puerto Rico. 5) Records of any complaints, concerns, or feedback received from the public or other agencies regarding the operation that took place on or around Sunday, January 26, 2024 across Puerto Rico. 6) Any other documents or materials related to the raid that took place on or around Sunday, January 26, 2024 across Puerto Rico. To aid in your search, please use the following keywords for each of the above-mentioned points; "Barrio Obrero" "dominicans" "dominicanos" "Buen Consejo" "haitianos" "indocumentados" "haitians" "documentos" "documentados" "ilegales" "ilegal" "illegals" "illegal aliens" "illegal" "San Juan" "Carolina" "migrants" "immigrants" "migrantes" "inmigrantes" This request is only for records produced from January 1, 2025 to the date this request is processed. Request for Expedited Processing: I respectfully request expedited processing of this FOIA request under 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(E) and 6 C.F.R. § 5.5(e). This request qualifies for expedited processing because there is a compelling need to inform the public about actual or alleged federal government activity, specifically the enforcement of immigration laws and the operations of federal agencies in Puerto Rico. The requested records pertain to matters of widespread and exceptional media interest and public concern, particularly regarding the enforcement of immigration rights and the conduct of federal agencies in Puerto Rico. Disclosure of these records will contribute significantly to public understanding of how federal immigration enforcement operations are conducted in Puerto Rico, a U.S. territory with unique legal and cultural considerations. Given the urgency of this issue, expedited processing is necessary to ensure timely dissemination of this information to the public. Urgency to Inform the Public: There is an urgent need to inform the public about the immigration enforcement operation conducted in the Barrio Obrero neighborhood of San Juan, Puerto Rico, on or about January 26, 2025. This operation has raised significant concerns among local communities, advocacy groups, and policymakers regarding the methods, scope, and impact of federal immigration enforcement in Puerto Rico. Expedited processing is necessary to ensure timely access to information that will allow the public to understand the nature of this operation and its implications for immigration rights and enforcement practices. Widespread and Exceptional Media Interest: The requested records pertain to a matter of widespread and exceptional media interest. Puerto Rico, as a U.S. territory, has a unique political and legal status that often results in heightened scrutiny of federal actions on the island. The immigration raid in Barrio Obrero has already garnered significant media attention, both locally and nationally, with numerous news outlets reporting on the operation and its impact on the community. Expedited processing will enable journalists and media organizations to provide accurate, timely, and comprehensive coverage of this issue, ensuring that the public is fully informed. Public Concern and Civic Engagement: The operation has sparked considerable public concern and debate about the role of federal agencies in enforcing immigration laws in Puerto Rico. Community organizations, legal advocates, and elected officials have called for greater transparency and accountability regarding the raid. Disclosure of the requested records will empower the public to engage in informed discussions about immigration policy, civil rights, and the treatment of Puerto Rico as a U.S. territory. Expedited processing is essential to facilitate this civic engagement and ensure that the public has access to critical information in a timely manner. Unique Legal and Cultural Context of Puerto Rico: Puerto Rico’s status as a U.S. territory creates a unique legal and cultural context for federal immigration enforcement. Unlike states, Puerto Rico does not have full representation in Congress, and its residents face distinct challenges in advocating for their rights. The requested records will shed light on how federal agencies operate in this unique context and whether their actions align with the principles of fairness, equity, and respect for local communities. Expedited processing is necessary to address these concerns promptly and ensure that the public has access to information about the federal government’s activities in Puerto Rico. Impact on Vulnerable Communities: Immigration enforcement operations often have a disproportionate impact on vulnerable communities, including low-income families, undocumented immigrants, and individuals with limited access to legal resources. The raid in Barrio Obrero has reportedly caused fear and uncertainty among residents, many of whom are concerned about potential violations of their rights. Disclosure of the requested records will help the public understand the scope and impact of the operation on these communities and assess whether federal agencies adhered to appropriate protocols and safeguards. Expedited processing is critical to addressing these concerns and ensuring accountability. Contribution to Public Understanding of Government Operations: The requested records will contribute significantly to public understanding of how federal agencies conduct immigration enforcement operations in Puerto Rico. This includes insights into the planning, execution, and aftermath of the raid, as well as the decision-making processes involved. By providing timely access to this information, expedited processing will enhance transparency and accountability, enabling the public to hold federal agencies accountable for their actions and advocate for necessary reforms. Precedent for Future Operations: The information contained in the requested records will have broader implications for future immigration enforcement operations in Puerto Rico and other U.S. territories. Understanding how federal agencies operate in Puerto Rico will help inform public policy debates and ensure that future actions are conducted in a manner that respects the rights and dignity of all individuals. Expedited processing is necessary to ensure that this information is available to policymakers, advocates, and the public before further operations are carried out. Fee Waiver Request: If any portion of the requested records is exempt from disclosure, I request that you redact only the exempt portions and release the remainder of the documents. If my request is denied in whole or in part, I ask that you provide a detailed justification for the denial, including reference to the specific FOIA exemption(s) relied upon, and inform me of my right to appeal the decision. I am willing to pay reasonable fees associated with processing this request, up to a maximum of $100.00. If the estimated fees exceed this limit, please notify me before proceeding. I am requesting a fee waiver as I am seeking this information in the public interest and not for commercial purposes. Disclosure of the requested records will contribute significantly to public understanding of government operations and activities, particularly regarding immigration enforcement practices in Puerto Rico. Please provide the requested records in electronic format, if possible. If you have any questions or need further clarification, you may contact me at [email] The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes. In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not. Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

response_text: Department of Energy Golden Field Office 15013 Denver West Parkway Golden, Colorado 80401 April 14, [address]. Boston, MA 02115 Via Email: [email] Re: GFO-2025-02855-F Dear Mr. Steinbaugh: This is the final response to the request for information that you sent to the Department of Energy (DOE)/Golden Field Office (GFO) under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), 5 U.S.C. § 552. You requested the following: "A copy of any FOIA request (including any request to expedite the request) in which a request to expedite production (on the basis of newsworthiness or an urgency to inform the public) was granted. This request is limited to requests received on or after January 1, 2024.” Assignment with Exemptions: Your request was assigned GFO’s Freedom of Information Act Office (FOIA). The search started on April 11, 2025, which is the cutoff date for responsive records. FOIA has completed its search and identified one (1) document responsive to your request. The documents are being released to you as described below. Upon review, GFO has determined that certain information should be withheld from the documents pursuant to Exemption 6 of the FOIA, 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(6). Exemption 6 Redactions within the responsive records were made pursuant to FOIA Exemption 6, which protects from disclosure all “personnel and medical files and similar files” when release of that information “would constitute a clearly unwarranted invasion of personal privacy.” Exemption 6 is intended to “protect individuals from the injury and embarrassment that can result from the unnecessary disclosure of personal information.” U.S. Dep't of State v. Washington Post Co., 456 U.S. 595, 599 (1982). Here, responsive documents to FOIA Request GFO-2025-02855-F contain names, addresses, and other personally identifiable information. Disclosure of this personal information would constitute a "clearly unwarranted invasion of personal privacy." U. S. Dep't of State v. Washington Post Co., 456 U.S. 595, 599 (1982). Release of this personal information would not further the public interest by shedding light on the operations or activities of the government. As this redacted information is of a personal nature, disclosure could reasonably result in harassment of the individuals and/or other unwarranted invasions of their personal privacy. It is DOE's conclusion in this case that the need to keep such personal information private outweighs any de minimis public interest gained from its release. For this reason, this personal information has been redacted. See e.g., U.S. v. Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press, 489 U.S. 749 (1989). This satisfies the standard set forth at 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(8)(A)(i) that agencies shall withhold information under FOIA “only if (I) the agency reasonably foresees that disclosure would harm an interest protected by an exemption…; or (II) disclosure is prohibited by law ….” 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(8)(A)(ii)(I) provides that whenever full disclosure of a record is not possible, agencies shall “consider whether partial disclosure of information is possible…and (III) take reasonable steps necessary to segregate and release nonexempt information.” Therefore, we have determined that, in certain instances, a partial disclosure is proper. Pursuant to 10 C.F.R. § 1004.7(b)(2), I am the individual responsible for the determination to withhold the information described above. The FOIA requires that “any reasonably segregable portion of a record shall be provided to any person requesting such record after deletion of the portions which are exempt.” 5 U.S.C. § 552(b). As a result, a redacted version of the documents is being released to you in accordance with 10 C.F.R. §1004.7(b)(3). Adequacy/Appeals: This determination, as well as the adequacy of the search, may be appealed within 90 calendar days from your receipt of this letter pursuant to 10 C.F.R. § 1004.8. Appeals should be addressed to Director, Office of Hearings and Appeals, HG-1, L’Enfant Plaza, U.S. Department of Energy, [address], S.W., Washington, D.C. 20585-1615. The written appeal, including the envelope, must clearly indicate that a FOIA appeal is being made. You may also submit your appeal to [email], including the phrase “Freedom of Information Appeal” in the subject line (this is the preferred method by the Office of Hearings and Appeals). The appeal must contain all of the elements required by 10 C.F.R. § 1004.8, including a copy of the determination letter. Thereafter, judicial review will be available to you in the Federal District Court either: 1) in the district where you reside; 2) where you have your principal place of business; 3) where DOE’s records are situated; or 4) in the District of Columbia. FOIA Liaison Officer/OGIS: You may contact GFO’s FOIA Public Liaison, Mark Barth, at [phone], or by email at [email], for any further assistance and to discuss any aspect of your request. Additionally, you may contact the Office of Government Information Services (OGIS) at the National Archives and Records Administration to inquire about the FOIA mediation services they offer. The contact information for OGIS is as follows: Office of Government Information Services, National Archives and Records Administration, [address]-OGIS, College Park, Maryland 20740-6001, e-mail at [email]; telephone at [phone]; toll free at [phone]; or facsimile at [phone]. response_text: U.S. Department of Justice Federal Bureau of Investigation Washington, D.C. 20535 October 20, 2025 MUCKROCK NEWS DEPT MR194590 263 HUNTINGTON AVENUE BOSTON, MA 02115 FOIPA Request No.: 1679479-002 Subject: KOHBERGER, BRYAN CHRISTOPHER (University of Idaho Murders/Investigation) Dear Rachael Morgan: This letter is in response to your Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request. The material responsive to your request is being withheld in full pursuant to Title 5, U.S. Code 552, subsections (b)(6), (b)(7)(C) and (b)(7)(A) [5 U.S.C. § 552 (b)(6), (b)(7)(C), (b)(7)(A)], and therefore your request is being closed. The records on third party individual(s) you requested are categorically denied pursuant to FOIA exemptions (b)(6) and (b)(7)(C), 5 U.S.C. §§ 552 (b)(6) and (b)(7)(C). Please be advised that you have not sufficiently demonstrated that the public’s interest in disclosure (relating to the operations and activities of the government) outweigh the personal privacy interests of an individual. While the existence of FBI records is acknowledged, the records are exempt from disclosure as processing these third party records would constitute an unwarranted invasion of personal privacy. Additionally, the material you requested is located in an investigative file which is exempt from disclosure pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(7)(A). 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(7)(A) exempts from disclosure: records or information compiled for law enforcement purposes, but only to the extent that the production of such law enforcement records or information ... could reasonably be expected to interfere with enforcement proceedings... The records responsive to your request are law enforcement records; there is a pending or prospective law enforcement proceeding relevant to these responsive records, and release of the information could reasonably be expected to interfere with enforcement proceedings. For a further explanation of this exemption, see the enclosed Explanation of Exemptions. Please refer to the enclosed

request_text: the following records: September 11, 2025 Food and Drug Administration Division of Freedom of Information (HFI-35) RE: Freedom of Information Act request Dear FOIA Officer: Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552a, I am requesting copies of the post-market biannual and/or annual safety reports that the FDA has required Eisai (Biogen as cooperating company) to submit to include data on ARIA,, brain hemorrhages and death s related to lecanemab/Leqembi (application 761269Orig1s000). The reports are required according to FDAAA Section 505(o)(3) and the first report would have been due in or around June 2023 and then annually in June 2024 and June 2025 (or thereabouts). However, it appears the reports might not have been submitted to the FDA as the PMR site at FDA states its status is "Delayed" and that "The final protocol submission was missed and the FDA issued a missed milestone letter on 04/21/2025, and a subsequent failure to respond letter on 06/17/2025." Since the above-mentioned reports are required, they should be easy to immediately identify electronically as PMR 4497-1 and to forward to us. If instead, the company has not submitted any reports, or omitted some reports, we request confirmation of that. We first requested these reports in November 2024. We believe the mortality data is critical for the public health and safety and hope the FDA will assist with obtaining their immediate release. I am a professional journalist writing with my colleague, Shannon Brownlee, for The BMJ and The Lever. As an investigative journalist, I request a waiver of fees associated with processing this FOIA request and duplicating responsive records. Thank you for your attention to this request.
response_text: February 18, 2026 Sending via Email: [email] Dear Requester: The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has completed processing of your September 11, 2025, Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request FDA-FOIA-2025-10861, in which you sought: ...I am requesting copies of the post-market biannual and/or annual safety reports that the FDA has required Eisai (Biogen as cooperating company) to submit to include data on ARIA,, brain hemorrhages and death s related to lecanemab/Leqembi (application 761269Orig1s000). The reports are required according to FDAAA Section 505(o)(3) and the first report would have been due in or around June 2023 and then annually in June 2024 and June 2025 (or thereabouts). However, it appears the reports might not have been submitted to the FDA as the PMR site at FDA states its status is "Delayed" and that "The final protocol submission was missed and the FDA issued a missed milestone letter on 04/21/2025, and a subsequent failure to respond letter on 06/17/2025." Since the above-mentioned reports are required, they should be easy to immediately identify electronically as PMR 4497-1 and to forward to us. If instead, the company has not submitted any reports, or omitted some reports, we request confirmation of that. We first requested these reports in November 2024. We believe the mortality data is critical for the public health and safety and hope the FDA will assist with obtaining their immediate release. Upon review, your request is denied in full. The information you have requested has been determined to be exempt under the following FOIA exemptions: 5 U.S.C. § 552 (b)(3), which allows us to withhold information that is specifically exempted from disclosure by statute, including Title VIII of Food and Drug Administration Amendments Act of 2007 ( 42 U.S.C. § 282(j)(6)), which permits the withholding of information submitted for publication on clinicaltrials.gov (or information of the same general nature or integrally associated with submitted information) that is not otherwise publicly available. (https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/USCODE-2011-title42/html/USCODE-2011-title42-chap6A-subchapIII-partA-sec282.htm) 5 U.S.C. § 552 (b)(4), which protects trade secrets and commercial or financial information obtained from a person and privileged or confidential. The following sections of the Health and Human Services (HHS) and FDA regulations are also applicable to this denial. 45 C.F.R. § 5.31(c), which authorizes our agency to withhold records which are specifically exempted from disclosure by statute. (https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-45/subtitle-A/subchapter-A/part-5#p-5.31(c)) 45 C.F.R. § 5.31(d), which authorizes our agency to withhold trade secrets and commercial or financial information obtained from a person and privileged or confidential. (https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-45/subtitle-A/subchapter-A/part-5#p-5.31(d)) 21 C.F.R. § 20.61, which protects trade secrets and commercial or financial information which is privileged or confidential. (https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-21/section-20.61) 21 C.F.R. § 601.50, which protects the confidentiality of data and information in an investigational new drug notice for a biological product. (https://www.ecfr.gov/current/title-21/chapter-I/subchapter-F/part-601/subpart-F/section-601.50) In accordance with 45 CFR § 5.61 and 21 CFR § 20.41(b)(5), you have the right to appeal this determination. By filing an appeal, you preserve your rights under FOIA and give the agency a chance to review and reconsider your request and the agency’s decision. Your appeal must be mailed or emailed within 90 days from the date of this response, to: Director, Office of Management and Enterprise Services US Food & Drug Administration 5630 Fishers Lane, Room 1050 Rockville, MD 20857 E-mail: [email] (mailto:[email]) Please clearly mark both the envelope and your letter “FDA Freedom of Information Act Appeal.” Your appeal should also reference your request number FDA-FOIA-2025-10861. Items arriving or delivered after 5 p.m. Eastern Time will be deemed received on the next workday. If you would like to discuss our response before filing an appeal to attempt to resolve your dispute without going through the appeals process, please feel free to contact me at [phone]. You may also contact the FDA FOIA Public Liaison for assistance at Office of Management and Enterprise Services US Food & Drug Administration 5630 Fishers Lane, Room 1050 Rockville, MD 20857 E-mail: [email]. (mailto:[email]) If you are unable to resolve your FOIA dispute through our FOIA Public Liaison, the Office of Government Information Services (OGIS), the Federal FOIA Ombudsman’s office, offers mediation services to help resolve disputes between FOIA requesters and Federal agencies. The contact information for OGIS is: Office of Government Information Services National Archives and Records Administration [address]–OGIS College Park, MD 20740-6001 Telephone [phone] Toll free [phone] Facsimile [phone] E-mail at [email]. (mailto:[email]) Sincerely yours, Charis Wilson, PhD, CRM Denials and Appeals Officer Division of Headquarters Freedom of Information Office of Management and Enterprise Services

You have requested records on one or more third party individuals. Pursuant to 28 C.F.R. § 16.8(a), your appeal must be postmarked or electronically transmitted within ninety (90) days of the date of this response to your request. If you submit your appeal by mail, both the letter and the envelope should be clearly marked "Freedom of Information Act Appeal." Please reference the FOIPA Request Number listed above in your correspondence so it may be easily identified. If possible, please provide a copy of your original request and this response letter with your appeal. Note: Utilizing the FBI’s dispute resolution services or requesting mediation through OGIS does not toll the ninety (90) day limit to file a timely appeal with OIP. Enclosed for your information is a copy of the Explanation of Exemptions. Sincerely, Amie M. Napier Section Chief Record/Information Dissemination Section Information Management Division Enclosure

request_text: the following records: I am seeking all records from 1940 through 1970 concerning Alva LaSalle “Beau” Kitselman, sometimes listed as A.L. Kitselman or Beau Kitselman. Kitselman was a mathematician, psychologist, and author known for his involvement in early cognitive research and associations with fringe scientific and metaphysical communities in California, Hawaii, and Nevada. Please include: • Any investigations, correspondence, memoranda, or references involving Kitselman. • Any files that mention his work with psychological, parapsychological, or psychedelics research, the Institute of Integration, E-Therapy, or Dianetics, or possible contacts with defense contractors, universities, or intelligence-linked research groups. • Any surveillance, background checks, or correspondence indexed to him under the Bureau’s Domestic Intelligence, COINTELPRO, or Scientific/Technical subfiles. Please search both main and cross-reference indices, including (but not limited to) the Los Angeles, San Diego, and Las Vegas Field Offices, as well as any Headquarters files related to scientific or psychological research programs. If responsive records have been destroyed or transferred, please indicate the date, authority, and disposition of such records. The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes. In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not. Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.
